metadata.summary: Identifies pathways customers use to discover and adopt a product: traffic-source mix across incumbents, partner/reseller/marketplace landscape, and benchmark channel economics with confidence bands.

content.title: Distribution Channel Research
content.summary: Distribution channel research identifies and evaluates the pathways through which customers discover and adopt a product at the market level: traffic-source mix across incumbents, partner/reseller/marketplace landscape, and benchmark channel economics (CPC/CPM/CAC ranges with confidence bands). It answers "how do people in this market actually find and buy products like this?" before committing GTM spend.

why.p: "If you build it, they will come" fails because founders pick channels based on preference rather than where the buyer segment actually lives. Market-level channel-share data (not guesswork) reveals which channels move volume, what the margin stack-up looks like through each path, and which partner types control access to the buyer — information that prevents wasted spend on wrong channels.

list.item: Market entry: need to know which channels actually move volume in a target market before committing GTM budget.
Investor/strategy decks requiring sourced channel-economics slide with market-level CPC/CAC ranges.
Channel-share studies: quantifying what % of category revenue flows through SEO vs paid vs partner vs direct.
Partner-landscape mapping for B2B: identifying distributors, resellers, MSPs, and integration partners.
Refreshing channel benchmarks when numbers in the deck are older than 12 months.
Pricing/business-model research that depends on channel margin stack-ups.

Market entry analysis: a client/founder needs to know which channels actually move volume in a target market before committing GTM spend (e.g. "is dental SaaS sold via direct, marketplace, or channel partners in DACH?").
Investor / strategy decks where the channel-economics slide must show market-level CPC/CAC ranges and partner take-rates with sourced citations, not back-of-envelope guesses.
Channel-share studies: quantifying what % of category revenue flows through SEO vs paid vs partner vs direct sales across the top 10 incumbents.
Partner-landscape mapping for B2B: identifying every distributor, reseller, MSP, marketplace, and integration partner that touches the buyer journey, with reach/exclusivity/take-rate per partner.
Pricing & business-model research that depends on channel margin stack-ups (e.g. SaaS direct 70% gross margin vs. AWS Marketplace listing −3% take vs. reseller −20%).
Refreshing channel benchmarks when incumbent CPC/CPM/CAC numbers in the deck are >12 months old.
Founder needs creative/copy/test-design help — route to growth-marketer.
Pre-PMF single-product validation where the question is "does anyone want this" — premature optimization.
Pure brand campaigns with no recurring channel decision.
Markets where channel data is structurally unavailable (private niches, NDAs, regulated verticals) — output will be conjecture; stop and flag.
When the requester has not specified a market boundary (geo + segment + buyer) — denominator is undefined.
Founder needs creative/copy/test-design help — that is a growth-marketing job, not market research; route to pro/marketing/growth-marketer or the researcher-variant of this methodology.
Pre-PMF single-product validation where the question is "does anyone want this" — channel research at that stage is premature optimization.
Pure brand campaigns or one-off launches with no recurring channel decision.
Markets where channel data is structurally unavailable (private niches, enterprise with NDAs, regulated verticals with no ad libraries) — output will be conjecture; flag and stop.
When the requester has not specified a market boundary (geo + segment + buyer); without a boundary the channel-share denominator is undefined.

rule.statement: Always research all four channel categories before selecting a primary: organic (SEO, content, social, community, WOM, PLG), paid (search ads, social ads, display, influencer, affiliate, sponsorships), sales-led (outbound, partnerships, conferences, webinars), and viral/network (referral, integrations, embeds, UGC).
rule.rationale: Founders pick channels based on preference, not fit. A channel chosen without reviewing the full taxonomy misses the category where buyers actually concentrate.

list.item: Organic: 6-12 months to results, low cost, sustainable growth, lower CAC over time
Paid: immediate results, scalable when proven, high cost, best for fast testing
Sales-led: high value B2B and complex products, 1-6 months to results, high cost
Viral/network: best for products with network effects, 1-4 months, low-variable cost

rule.statement: Score each candidate channel on six weighted factors: audience presence (25%), competitors using (15%), cost to test (20%), time to results (15%), scalability (15%), team capability (10%). Prioritize highest-scoring channels for initial investment.
rule.rationale: Weighting audience presence highest prevents the common failure of investing in channels where the target buyer does not exist, regardless of team familiarity.

rule.statement: A channel is viable only when LTV:CAC exceeds 3:1. Model channel economics (CPM → CTR → CPC → conversion rate → CAC) before scaling any channel beyond micro-test budget.
rule.rationale: Channels that feel productive but have LTV:CAC under 3:1 are capital destroyers — they feel like growth while compounding a structural margin problem.

rule.statement: Follow the five-step testing sequence: micro-test ($100-500, 1-2 weeks) → validate signal → scale test ($1-5K, 1 month) → optimize conversion → scale budget. Never jump from micro-test to full scale.
rule.rationale: Skipping the scale-test stage bets the marketing budget on a statistically insignificant micro-test; the validate step decides whether to proceed.

rule.statement: Portfolio approach by stage: at 0-$10K MRR master 1 channel; at $10-50K MRR add 2-3 channels; at $50K+ MRR diversify. Maintain 80% effort on 1-2 proven channels, 20% on experiments.
rule.rationale: Trying all channels simultaneously dilutes effort below the minimum needed to get accurate signal from any one channel.

example.note: B2B SaaS tool: interviews show 60% discover via Google search, 25% via peer recommendations, 15% via LinkedIn. Strategy: SEO primary (content for "how to [problem]" keywords), LinkedIn secondary (thought leadership + ads), referral tertiary (in-product program). SEO CAC after 6 months: $20; LinkedIn CAC: $150; referral CAC: $30.

Consumer mobile app: 45% from App Store search, 30% from friend recommendation, 25% from Instagram/TikTok. Strategy: ASO primary (free organic installs), viral loops secondary (shareable content), TikTok organic tertiary. Avoids paid channels until virality loop is proven.

section.name: four-stage-pipeline

rule.statement: Always run the boundary agent first and refuse to proceed until geo + segment + buyer + product category are locked. An unbounded market produces meaningless channel-share denominators.
rule.rationale: Channel-share is a fraction — without a denominator (market boundary) it is undefined. Agents that skip this gate produce plausible-sounding numbers with no anchor.

list.item: Stage 1 (Boundary agent): lock geo + segment + buyer + category → market-definition spec
Stage 2 (Channel-share agent): quantify traffic/revenue mix across top 10 incumbents using SimilarWeb + Ahrefs + ad libraries → market-level channel-share table with confidence bands
Stage 3 (Partner-landscape agent): map every distributor/reseller/marketplace/integration partner with reach, exclusivity, take-rate → partner-landscape table
Stage 4 (Economics agent): layer benchmark CPC/PMC/CAC on channel-share table, run LTV sensitivity (low/mid/high) → LTV:CAC ranges per channel

section.note: Drive market-level channel research with a 4-stage chain. (1) Boundary agent locks geo + segment + buyer + product category and produces a market-definition spec — refuse to proceed without it. (2) Channel-share agent quantifies traffic/revenue mix across the top 10 incumbents using SimilarWeb + Ahrefs + ad libraries, emits a market-level channel-share table with confidence bands. (3) Partner-landscape agent maps every distributor / reseller / marketplace / integration partner, with reach, exclusivity, and known/inferred take-rate. (4) Economics agent layers benchmark CPC/CPM/CAC on the channel-share table and runs LTV sensitivity (low/mid/high) to produce LTV:CAC ranges per channel.

rule.statement: Report channel-share as a range with confidence band tied to N of competitors with available data: HIGH if ≥7 incumbents agree, MED if 4-6, LOW if ≤3. Never output a single percentage without the band.
rule.rationale: SimilarWeb traffic mix is a proxy for channel-share, not a measurement of revenue-share — a single number projects false precision onto an inherently noisy estimate.

rule.statement: Use revenue- or traffic-weighted aggregation when computing market-level channel-share — never equal-weight across competitors. Weight by SimilarWeb monthly visits as proxy.
rule.rationale: Equal-weight averaging gives micro-competitors the same influence as category leaders, skewing the picture toward the long tail.

rule.statement: Report partner take-rates as facts when from public marketplace docs (AWS 3%, Shopify 0-20%, App Store 15-30%) and as [INFERRED] with inference path when from job posts or partner-portal scrapes.
rule.rationale: Private reseller margins (15-40%) are negotiated and only leak via indirect signals; treating inferred rates as facts leads to materially wrong economics models.

rule.statement: Every number must carry source + URL + capture-date triple. Economic numbers older than 12 months must be marked [UNVERIFIED] rather than cited as current.
rule.rationale: Ad library data and traffic estimates rot in weeks; stale benchmarks in investor decks create credibility risk when scrutinized.

rule.statement: Run LTV sensitivity at low/mid/high LTV and output ranges, not point estimates. Reject any single LTV:CAC ratio output as incomplete.
rule.rationale: LTV uncertainty kills market-level LTV:CAC modeling — the right output is a range with sensitivity bands so investors and operators can see the cliff.

rule.statement: Always include regional splits for multi-geo markets. Never blend global averages — CPC variance across regions can be 3-5x, destroying the strategic recommendation.
rule.rationale: US-centric benchmarks systematically misrepresent APAC and EU markets where CPCs, partner ecosystems, and channel maturity differ fundamentally.

antipattern.description: Trying all channels simultaneously at launch.
antipattern.reason: Dilutes effort below the minimum needed to get accurate signal from any single channel. Focus on 1-2 initially.

antipattern.description: Copying competitor channels without validating fit for the specific product.
antipattern.reason: Channel fit depends on audience presence, cost structure, and team capability — not on what the market leader does.

antipattern.description: Ignoring channel economics before scaling — no CAC calculation before increasing budget.
antipattern.reason: Channels that feel productive but have LTV:CAC under 3:1 destroy capital; economics must be modeled before scaling.

antipattern.description: Giving up on a channel after fewer than 4-6 weeks with no optimization.
antipattern.reason: Organic channels take 3-12 months to mature; premature abandonment produces false negatives that leave viable channels unexplored.

antipattern.description: No attribution tracking set up before running channel tests.
antipattern.reason: Without attribution, test results cannot be attributed to any channel; the data is useless for decision-making.

antipattern.description: Channel granularity collapse: "paid social" lumped together as one channel in the market-share table.
antipattern.reason: "Paid social" is not one channel — Meta Reels, Meta Feed, IG Stories, LinkedIn Sponsored, TikTok In-Feed behave differently. Force per-platform breakdown or the analysis is meaningless for channel selection.

antipattern.description: Survivorship in published case studies: "we got to $10M via SEO" stories overweight organic and underweight paid.
antipattern.reason: Published stories are 1-of-100 — channel-share inference from public stories must be cross-checked with SimilarWeb traffic mix data from the silent majority.

antipattern.description: Producing a 7-tier portfolio recommendation ("Phase 1 SEO, Phase 2 paid") in market-research output.
antipattern.reason: GTM sequencing is GTM-strategist work, not market research. Stop at channel-share + partner-landscape + economics tables; let the strategist interpret them.

antipattern.description: Agents skipping the boundary gate and producing "channel mix for SaaS" without geo or segment.
antipattern.reason: Without a market boundary the channel-share denominator is undefined and all output is conjecture. Enforce a stop-and-ask gate before any data work.

antipattern.description: Agents fabricating CPC, CPM, take-rate, and traffic-share values without sourced citations.
antipattern.reason: Number hallucination in channel benchmarks produces investment decisions grounded in fiction. Require source + URL + capture-date for every number and reject answers failing the citation check.

antipattern.description: Boundary skipping: agents will happily produce "channel mix for SaaS" without a geo or segment.
antipattern.reason: Enforce a stop-and-ask gate before any data work; without a market boundary the channel-share denominator is undefined and all output is conjecture.

antipattern.description: Number hallucination: agents fabricate plausible CPC, CPM, take-rate, and traffic-share values.
antipattern.reason: Require source + URL + capture-date for every number, and reject answers that fail the citation check.

antipattern.description: US-bias: training data overweights US benchmarks; for non-US markets force the agent to source regional data.
antipattern.reason: For non-US markets force the agent to source regional data (Statista, eMarketer regional, local Ad libraries) rather than defaulting to US benchmarks.

antipattern.description: Confidence-band omission: agents collapse channel-share into single percentages.
antipattern.reason: Explicitly require N + confidence band per row; a single percentage projects false precision onto an inherently noisy estimate.

antipattern.description: Survivorship lean: agents will overweight famous "we won via SEO" stories.
antipattern.reason: Prompt for the silent majority (incumbents that never wrote a Medium post) by sampling top-10 by traffic, not top-10 by content output.

antipattern.description: Partner-landscape under-reporting: agents miss MSPs, VARs, and SI partners because those rarely have web presence.
antipattern.reason: Require the agent to scrape "Partners / Resellers / Find a partner" pages of each incumbent and capture every named entity.

antipattern.description: Equal-weight aggregation bug: agents default to averaging across competitors equally.
antipattern.reason: Explicitly demand traffic- or revenue-weighted aggregation; equal weights give micro-competitors the same influence as category leaders.

antipattern.description: LTV point-estimate bug: agents output a single LTV:CAC ratio.
antipattern.reason: Require low/mid/high sensitivity or reject the answer; point estimates hide the cliff where unit economics invert.
Require capture-date >= last 12 months for any economic number, else mark [UNVERIFIED].

list.item: Channel-share denominators are noisy: SimilarWeb traffic mix is a proxy for channel-share, not a measurement of revenue-share; for B2B with 6-month sales cycles the gap is enormous.

Partner take-rates are rarely public — public marketplace fees (AWS 3%, Shopify 0–20%, App Store 15–30%) are anchors, but private reseller margins (15–40%) are negotiated and leak only via job posts, RFPs, and partner-portal scrapes.

Ad-library coverage is uneven: Meta + Google have full transparency in EU/Brazil, partial in US; LinkedIn + TikTok ad libraries are shallow; B2B podcast and newsletter sponsorship has no library at all.
Survivorship in case studies: published "we got to $10M via SEO" stories are 1-of-100 — channel-share inference from public stories overweights organic and underweights paid.
LTV uncertainty kills market-level LTV:CAC modeling — the right output is a range with sensitivity bands, not a point estimate, and agents that produce point estimates are silently wrong.
Geographic averaging: blending US + EU + APAC CPC into one number masks 5x regional variance that destroys the strategic recommendation.

"Channel" is fractal: "paid social" is not one channel — Meta Reels, Meta Feed, IG Stories, LinkedIn Sponsored, LinkedIn InMail behave differently. Pick the right granularity for the market question or the analysis is meaningless.
Lock the market boundary (geo + segment + buyer + category) before any channel work; an unbounded market produces meaningless channel-share.

Use weighted aggregation when computing market-level channel-share: weight each competitor by revenue or traffic (proxy: SimilarWeb monthly visits) — not equal weights — so micro-competitors do not skew the picture.
Capture every public channel datapoint with source + URL + capture-date triple; ad libraries and traffic estimates rot in weeks.
Report channel-share as a range with confidence band (HIGH/MED/LOW) tied to N of competitors with available data, not a single number.
Always include regional splits for multi-geo markets — global averages hide 3–5x CPC variance that breaks the strategic recommendation.

For partner take-rates: report public marketplace fees as facts; report reseller / channel-partner margins as [INFERRED] with the inference path (job post wording, RFP leak, partner-portal screenshot).
Treat channel-share + partner-landscape outputs as dated snapshots, not standing truth — refresh quarterly for active markets, semi-annually for slow markets.
Use LTV sensitivity (low/mid/high) bands not point estimates — investors and operators need to see the cliff.
Cross-check ad-library evidence with traffic mix: a competitor with 0% paid in SimilarWeb but 200 active Meta ads is a measurement artifact, not a contradiction — flag it.
Separate buy-side channels (where the buyer searches) from fulfillment channels (how the product is delivered) — for B2B SaaS sold via AWS Marketplace, the channel-share table needs both rows.

Avoid the 7-tier portfolio recommendation cliché ("Phase 1 SEO, Phase 2 paid…") in market-research output — that is GTM-strategist work, not market research; stop at the channel-share + partner-landscape + economics tables.
faion-research-agent (mode competitors) — pulls per-competitor traffic mix, ad creatives, partner mentions, marketplace listings.
faion-research-agent (mode niche) — produces market boundary + buyer-journey scaffold for stage 1.
faion-research-agent (mode pricing) — feeds margin / take-rate context for partner-economics math.
faion-domain-checker-agent — cheap pre-flight when partner / marketplace listings imply microsite domains for verification.
pro/research/market-researcher/competitor-analysis knowledge — per-competitor channel inference.
pro/research/market-researcher/market-research-tam-sam-som knowledge — sets the revenue denominator that channel-share is computed against.
pro/marketing/ppc-manager knowledge — current paid-channel CPC/CPM/CAC benchmarks with regional splits.
pro/marketing/growth-marketer knowledge — LTV modeling and sensitivity bands.
pro/marketing/gtm-strategist knowledge — interprets channel-share output for portfolio recommendations.

section.name: prompt-pattern
section.prompt: Role: market-level distribution-channel research subagent (market-researcher). Inputs: market_boundary={geo, segment, buyer, category}, incumbents=[top10_domains], LTV_low/mid/high=[$X,$Y,$Z], analysis_depth={share|partners|economics|all}. Tasks: 1. Verify market boundary is well-defined; if any field is missing, STOP and request it. 2. For each incumbent: extract traffic-source mix (cite SimilarWeb date), paid-ad presence (cite ad-library date), partner/marketplace listings. 3. Aggregate to market-level channel-share table with confidence bands (HIGH if >=7 incumbents agree, MED if 4-6, LOW if <=3). 4. Map partner landscape: distributors, resellers, marketplaces, integrations. For each: reach (S/M/L), exclusivity (Y/N), take-rate (cite or [INFERRED]). 5. Layer benchmark CPC/CPM/CAC ranges per channel (cite source + date, regional split if multi-geo). 6. Run LTV:CAC at low/mid/high LTV, output ranges not points. Constraints: - Every number cites source + capture date or is flagged [UNVERIFIED]. - No time estimates; complexity tags only (low/med/high). - If <3 incumbents have data, return LOW-confidence and recommend primary research. Return: markdown matching templates.md sections 1-5.

template.purpose: Channel research report skeleton: discovery, evaluation, economics, competitor channels, testing plan
Per-channel test report: parameters, metrics table, analysis, recommendation
Aggregate per-competitor traffic mixes into market-level share table with confidence bands
Partner-landscape table skeleton with reach, exclusivity, take-rate, source, capture-date columns
